title: Age pension may be next on the hit list
full_text:
A push for “intergenerational fairness” has underpinned the government’s sweeping changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing, but left alone to date is the most obvious target: access to the age pension. Will it be the next domino to fall?
Getting the pension – or a part pension – has always been useful. But more recently the fine art of pension access has become a key part of financial planning advice, especially to those in the “sweet spot” – the zone where you can access the pension and still have significant investments in superannuation.
Crucially, this advice invariably pivots on the last great tax shelter in Australia – the family home, where the value of your primary residence is effectively exempt from the means test for pension access.
That goes a long way towards explaining why the majority of retirees – about 2.7 million people – get some form of age pension.
As Centaur Financial Services’ Hugh Robertson has previously explained, “the sweet spot is in the region of $600,000-$800,000, where a couple can have super and get a pension”.
And who would not want a reliable income stream if they could get it? In fact, it’s even better than it sounds because the age pension is a government-guaranteed income stream that gets indexed for inflation twice a year.
At rock bottom, that income stream, excluding extra benefits, for a couple is worth $47,000 a year.
Think of this way: If you don’t have a government pension and assume you are investing at the best deposit rates in the market, which are close to 5 per cent, then you would need about $940,000 to get a return of $47,000.
Ironically, it is the arrival of a new pension benefit that has piqued interest once more on the politically sensitive issue of pension access: The new rules on capital gains tax impose a minimum 30 per cent tax rate for everyone, except pension recipients.
And you don’t have to get much of a pension to get that exemption. The only criteria is that you get something.
For example, if you get $100 a year in a part pension, you get a full CGT exemption.
No wonder the policy analysts are on the case.
The most significant move so far has come from Policy Institute Australia, a think tank run by well-regarded economist Amy Auster, ex-ANZ and PwC.
The institute has released a report arguing that pension access should be trimmed, with the value of the family home brought into the means test as a key measure.
In turn, the report suggests the government could save $10bn. As Auster says: “Under the current system, you can have a $5m house and a super balance of around $300,000 and get a full age pension.
“It is simply not equitable; this is not what the system was designed to do. “So what we are suggesting is that any equity held in the home above $500,000 is included in the assets test.” The report says means-testing rules have not kept pace with a wealthier and older Australia. The report found that the top 20 per cent most well-off houses received about 20 per cent of the expansion in pension programs.
Auster argues that such a measure would eliminate the exceptional distortion in the pension system where older Australians who rent get a less generous deal from the pension than those who own their own house.
The proposal – which is linked with an opt-in “retirement contribution scheme” and efforts to ensure aged care access would not be negatively affected – is included in the report titled “Home Truths: The case for rebalancing towards better means testing”.
Last year it was reported that advisers to Social Services Minister Tanya Plibersek had recommended that the pension access rules be revised to eliminate investors from exploiting current arrangements.
Among the economic modelling done for the department is a reduction on the pension asset test by about $100,000, a move that would cut thousands of older Australians out of the age pension stream.
Put these initiatives together and it is clear that means testing, especially for the pension access, is low-hanging fruit for the government in its next phase of achieving “intergenerational fairness”. If your long-term financial plans revolve around current rules for pension access, let’s put it this way: “regulatory risk” is staring you straight in the face.
